March 2026 roadmap

Slashpresent Improvement Roadmap

Enhancing agentic slide development with a stronger visual system, reusable consulting-grade components, and a more intentional default deck surface.

Core engine Reveal.js foundation Focus aesthetics and components Excludes LLM architecture changes
Executive summary

What changes the product fastest

Slashpresent already has the right substrate. The fastest route to a materially better product is to upgrade the default theme, add a serious component language, and make the shipped deck feel authored instead of template-generated.

4 priority phases
10+ new visual primitives
1 clear aesthetic direction
0 AI-slop defaults
Current state

What already works

  • Reveal.js gives you navigation, notes, transitions, and export-friendly structure.
  • The repo already supports a themed presentation workflow instead of raw HTML chaos.
  • The architecture is lightweight enough to stay editable and transparent.
  • A curated default deck can become the product's design reference instead of documentation drift.

What holds it back

  • The stock Scout branding makes the repo feel inherited, not owned.
  • The component vocabulary is too thin for consulting, strategy, or roadmap work.
  • The default deck demonstrates Reveal features, not Slashpresent taste.
  • There is no premium visual baseline for agents to imitate slide after slide.
Competitive lens

Where Slashpresent should differentiate

Dimension Current Slashpresent Leading tools Immediate repo response
Default look Generic template styling Confident visual identity from slide one Ship a premium default theme and showcase deck
Component range Basic cards and utility styles Purpose-built business layouts Add matrices, comparison tables, timelines, proof bands, quote blocks
Narrative quality Structure is manual and inconsistent Slides reinforce storyline shape Embed narrative-ready sections into the starter deck
Brand ownership Still reads as Scout Product identity is unmistakable Rebrand the main surface to Slashpresent
Visual system

A more intentional aesthetic baseline

Editorial typography

Fraunces for hierarchy, Open Runde Sans for body, IBM Plex Mono for data and code.

Dark paper palette

Graphite surfaces, warm paper text, copper highlight, and eucalyptus secondary accents.

Framed surfaces

Every important layout lives inside a designed shell, not loose floating text.

Rule no purple-on-white defaults Rule one accent per surface Rule components first, inline styles last
Component library

Consulting-grade layout primitives

Stat row

Dense KPI strips for executive summaries and proof slides.

Proof band

Compact evidence tags for claims, metrics, and source-backed signals.

Comparison table

Cleaner side-by-side framing for competitors, options, or requirements.

Timeline

Readable sequencing for execution plans, migrations, and rollouts.

"The goal is not more slide chrome. The goal is a default deck that looks like it was built by someone with taste and a point of view."
Slashpresent design principle
Structured thinking

New matrices make strategic slides easier to author

Foundation

  • Slashpresent theme
  • Shell, chip, card, proof primitives
  • Default roadmap deck

Content systems

  • Narrative templates
  • Grounding-aware layouts
  • Speaker note structure

Delivery

  • PPTX export fidelity
  • PDF polish
  • Theme variants

Automation surface

  • Template extraction
  • Programmatic composition
  • Agent workflow hooks
Theme variants

One product, multiple credible moods

Slashpresent

Editorial dark, warm paper text, copper accents.

Executive

Navy base with restrained gold for board and finance narratives.

Minimal

Paper-led layout for research, education, and quiet technical decks.

Nature

Muted green and stone for sustainability and long-horizon systems work.

Execution roadmap

Prioritized repo work

Phase 1

Visual ownership

Rebrand the default deck, ship the new theme, and establish premium shells, cards, and typographic rules.

Phase 2

Component depth

Expand matrices, comparison modules, timelines, quote blocks, and dashboard rows until strategy decks feel first-class.

Phase 3

Template intelligence

Focus on import/export and template extraction workflows, not generic AI generation surfaces.

Phase 4

Variant system

Add credible theme families and richer component combinations so agents can compose distinct decks without aesthetic drift.

Closing principle

Make the default output feel authored

The biggest improvement is not a bigger stack. It is a stronger visual point of view and a more complete component language inside the repo agents already use.

Immediate better theme and components Near-term richer templates and export fidelity Outcome decks that feel deliberate, not generated
